analysis:
The stronger heuristics share a clear structure: prune dominated candidates, create a single shared Monte‑Carlo sample set, precompute point→sample domination masks in memory‑safe blocks, run a lazy‑greedy selection (max‑heap) with incremental overlap subtraction, and then polish a small shortlist with bounded exact HV swaps only when affordable. Key efficiency wins come from reusing a single sample set and masks, converting per‑point masks to sparse index lists when coverage is sparse, reducing samples to only "relevant" covered ones, and capping polish iteration/shortlist size.

Weaker designs repeatedly compute exact hypervolumes inside hot loops (full recomputation per candidate or per swap), rebuild hypervolume objects, or use heavy global stochastic search without reusing intermediate data. These incur high CPU and memory costs. Common avoidable overheads: not blocking boolean matrix computations (risking memory spikes), not reducing to relevant samples, excessive MC sample sizes unrelated to k and D, and unbounded exact‑HV polishing. Trade‑offs are clear: MC approximations with careful sample sizing + lazy greedy give most of the HV gain cheaply; a tiny, bounded exact refinement recovers remaining quality with few expensive calls.
experience: Always prune dominated points, reuse one MC sample set and precomputed masks (use sparse lists when sparse), perform lazy greedy with incremental overlap updates, and limit exact HV calls to a small shortlisted pool with capped iterations. Tune sample count adaptively with k and D and block boolean work to be memory‑safe. Avoid recomputing hypervolume objects inside inner loops.
current_reflection: Always prune dominated points, reuse one MC sample set and precomputed masks (use sparse lists when sparse), perform lazy greedy with incremental overlap updates, and limit exact HV calls to a small shortlisted pool with capped iterations. Tune sample count adaptively with k and D and block boolean work to be memory‑safe. Avoid recomputing hypervolume objects inside inner loops.
bad_reflections: Prune dominated points early, reuse one MC sample set and block‑computed boolean masks, and drive selection with a lazy max‑heap of marginal gains (incrementally subtract overlaps). Limit exact hypervolume calls to a small shortlisted set and cap polish iterations. Vectorize updates, avoid per‑candidate full HV recomputations, and use box‑volume tie‑breakers and deterministic seeds for stable, fast heuristics.

Prune dominated/ref‑worse points, use one shared MC sample set computed in memory‑safe blocks, precompute boolean masks, run lazy greedy via a max‑heap with incremental overlap updates, tie‑break by cheap box‑volumes, and do a bounded exact polish on a small shortlist. Cap samples/iterations, vectorize inner work, reuse hypervolume objects, and seed RNGs for reproducibility.

Prune dominated points, build one shared MC sample set in memory‑safe blocks, precompute boolean domination masks, use a lazy greedy heap with incremental overlap updates, then polish only a small shortlist with exact HV. Cache hypervolume objects, scale sample count with k and D, and cap refinement iterations. These give best quality per runtime.
comprehensive_reflection:
• Keywords: prune dominated points, shared MC sample set, precomputed sparse masks, lazy greedy updates, incremental overlap, capped exact hypervolume calls.  
• Advice: Reuse one MC sample set with precomputed (sparse when applicable) masks, perform lazy greedy selection with incremental overlap updates, and adapt sample counts based on k and D while processing in memory‑safe blocks.  
• Avoid: Recomputing hypervolume objects within inner loops and over-evaluating large candidate pools with full HV calls.  
• Explanation: This approach maximizes efficiency by early pruning and resource reuse, minimizes redundant computations, and limits costly exact evaluations within a scalable heuristic framework.
